evidence_quotes.quote: A Game with direct or indirect online dependencies should be legally obligated to release fully offline usable and playable Versions at no extra Cost to the consumer, once the End of Life is reached, and Server infrastructure is shut down.
Remote Kill switches in the way we are seeing in Physical Products like the Nintendo Switch 2, should be prohibited.
The liberal usage of so called "Kernel Anti-Cheat" systems by the video game industry is another major concern and should be addressed.

paragraphs.text: The Digital Fairness Act is a good step in the right Direction for Consumer Protection and Market Oversight in the EU. However there are some Areas where it at the moment is Lacking proper objective, mainly in the Area of Digital Games, which constitute a massive Market and to this day is largely unregulated and very hostile to consumers. I suggest specific amendments which are both easy to enforce and follow by Companies. 1. A Game with direct or indirect online dependencies should be legally obligated to release fully offline usable and playable Versions at no extra Cost to the consumer, once the End of Life is reached, and Server infrastructure is shut down. Where technically possible. Which it would be for most Products as offline copies without DRM will always exist somewhere for Debugging, and Quality Assurance purposes. This should include the removal of any server based DRM, which also would be technically trivial for any studio utilizing these DRMs in the first place. 2. Remote Kill switches in the way we are seeing in Physical Products like the Nintendo Switch 2, should be prohibited. These modern "kill switches" do not just remove the access to online services which can be permittable to ensure Fairplay within these Games, however as of recent the physical hardware is disabled remotely arbitrarily, sometimes just for non harmful Profile names. This is sort of centralized Control over bought and owned hardware is a worrying trend the EU should address, better sooner than later. 3. The liberal usage of so called "Kernel Anti-Cheat" systems by the video game industry is another major concern and should be addressed. These Systems which essentially allow 3rd Party applications and code to run at the highest privilege level inside a Computer, don't only cause major Consumer hardship, like false positives in detection, reduced compatibility with other Platforms and potentially dangerous levels of Control over an Operating System, but also much more worryingly present a major easily exploitable attack vector on hundreds of millions of European Computers. It is a documented practice to exploit these Kernel level Drivers used to facilitate Anti Cheat and others. Any error in any of these System constitutes a massive security problem, that is both incredibly wide reaching and virtually undetectable. Something which already happened in a numerous cases, and is bound to happen again. Anti Cheat software remains necessary of course, but there are alternatives to putting it into the Kernel, that can be as effective as the current status quo. Proper investigation into the matter and weighing of the consequences and alternatives should be a priority for the Comission especially in the age of digital and hybrid warfare. This also shouldn't be limited to video games, as an issue in a Kernel Driver (the same methodology Kernel Anti Cheat relies on) was directly responsible for the disastrous 2024 crowd strike outtage which had massive impacts on both European infrastructure and the economy.
